decomposition.title: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing, enterprise IT, hospitality, digital innovation, and infrastructure hosting
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget stated. Inferred target opportunity approximately $15M-$50M over 3-5 years for enterprise-scale hosting/DCaaS/cloud/hybrid transformation and related setup, hosting, migration/implementation, consulting, support, and managed services pricing; Phase 0 evaluation itself is not stated as a committed contract.
decomposition.contract_duration: Not explicitly stated. Inferred likely 3-5 years for any resulting enterprise hosting/DCaaS/cloud arrangement based on large-scale data center exit, SAP/non-SAP hosting, resilience, DR, managed services, and cost-efficiency requirements; current RFP itself is a Phase 0 evaluation with proposals due 26 June 2026 and no committed execution term.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate a proposed target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must cover the landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must cover environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must cover identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must cover the hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must cover region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency appears as one bullet in the technical scope rather than as a detailed legal clause; vendors must still address it explicitly.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Region strategy and data residency approach required; no specific regions or residency restrictions are stated.

requirements.text: Vendors must cover disaster recovery, high availability and scalability patterns.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios including Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid where applicable and clearly state assumptions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP allows Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid scenarios where applicable, so vendors must be explicit about which scenarios they are answering and assumptions for each.

requirements.text: Architecture diagrams, reference designs and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
A detailed inventory of in-scope applications and infrastructure was attached in the RFP package online and provides counts and sizing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The detailed inventory was provided separately online and is not contained in the main RFP text; failure to use it will undermine sizing and pricing.

requirements.text: In-scope services include enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center, COTS and internally developed applications, Windows and Linux virtual machines, databases, middleware, file storage, backup and DR, IAM integrations, network connectivity, and monitoring/logging/security tooling.
Selected OT-adjacent workloads are in scope, limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: OT-adjacent workloads are easy to overlook in a hosting RFP but are expressly limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset and use it as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory baseline is an external dependency; response sizing, design, and pricing must be tied to it.

requirements.text: Vendors must highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Optional services must be separated; bundling extras into base scope may impair comparability or pricing evaluation.

requirements.text: Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Proposal submission instructions are a classic administrative disqualifier; the document requires submission within the defined timeline and through the designated channel.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple Yes/No answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: A bare compliance matrix with Yes/No answers is expressly insufficient and may fail before meaningful technical scoring.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the Q&A template provided.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Clarification process uses a required template and strict timeline; missed clarification deadlines may prevent issue resolution before submission.

requirements.text: All communication related to this RFP should be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Communications are restricted to named email channels; use of other routes may create process non-compliance.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The mandatory response template is an external artifact; missing it or using another format can make the proposal non-comparable.

requirements.text: Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Certification evidence is mandatory but buried in Compliance & Regulatory Requirements without naming the exact standards.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are referenced but not enumerated.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required; specific certifications not named in the main RFP.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a provider-type-specific compliance requirement and refers bidders to the Vendor Response template for more information.
requirements.cert_required: Compliance attestations as per industry standards for DCaaS providers.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of this RFP and the inventory baseline provided.

Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including foundational setup costs, application and infrastructure hosting costs, migration/implementation costs if applicable, and any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Pricing must cover cost categories beyond the Phase 0 evaluation, including hosting, migration/implementation if applicable, consulting, support, and managed services.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: SAP cost separation is specifically required and can expose cost-driver transparency issues.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency requirement is easy to miss in pricing instructions and can make commercial submissions non-comparable.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Kohler expects cost reductions or limited increases over time based on prices paid, not generic list-price discounts.

requirements.text: Payment terms are an important part of the proposal; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and proposals should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Net 90 payment terms are a commercial landmine: anything less is expressly stated as not competitive, and proposals should contemplate no less than net 90.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model aligned to the solution being proposed.
The governance overview should include an indicative governance structure and engagement model.
The governance overview should include reporting and review mechanisms such as periodic status updates and performance tracking.
The governance overview should include an escalation framework for issue resolution.
The governance overview should include key roles and responsibilities at a high level.
Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Confidentiality appears in the appendix; breach of use restrictions can create legal and procurement risk.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Third-party disclosure restriction is in the appendix and can affect subcontractor, hyperscaler, and partner sharing during solution development.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Appendix compliance language requires capability against applicable standards and data protection obligations, while detailed terms are deferred.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.

ambiguities: No explicit budget or estimated contract value is stated in the RFP. estimated_deal_size_usd is inferred from the large enterprise hosting/DCaaS/cloud scope, SAP and non-SAP workloads, data center exit evaluation, and required pricing for hosting, migration, consulting, support, and managed services.

No explicit contract term or renewal structure is stated. contract_duration is inferred as a likely 3-5 year hosting/transformation commercial horizon, while the current document is explicitly a Phase 0 evaluation and not a commitment to execution.
The document does not explicitly say late proposals will be rejected, but it states proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline and the response due date is 26 June.
The document does not explicitly say failure to use the Vendor Response template will result in rejection, but it states vendors are required to submit responses using the template.
The document states simple Yes/No answers will not be considered sufficient, but does not define whether insufficiency causes automatic disqualification or scoring penalty.
Payment terms below net 90 are stated as not competitive, but the RFP does not explicitly say they are automatically rejected.
Detailed requirements, response structure, evaluation criteria, scoring model, inventory counts, and compliance details are in external Excel/inventory templates not included in this document.
Applicable industry/regional standards and required certifications are not named in the main RFP; vendors must determine and evidence applicable certifications.
Detailed legal terms, contractual conditions, compliance requirements, SLAs, and service delivery frameworks will be defined in subsequent phases, creating future-contracting uncertainty.
hard_disqualifiers: Proposal submission timing/channel risk: proposals "must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel" and RFP responses are due 26 June. See [PAGE 8] and [PAGE 9].
Mandatory response-template risk: vendors are "required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template" provided with the RFP. See [PAGE 9].
Insufficient-response risk: "simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient." See [PAGE 8].
Certification-evidence risk: vendors "must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards" and "Evidence of certifications required." See [PAGE 7].
DCaaS-specific compliance-attestation risk: "DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards." See [PAGE 7].
Pricing-currency risk: "All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD)." See [PAGE 9].
compliance_red_flags: Evidence of certifications is required for compliance with applicable industry/regional standards, but the specific certifications are not listed in the main RFP. See [PAGE 7].
DCaaS providers have an explicit additional compliance-attestation requirement referenced to the Vendor Response template. See [PAGE 7].
Vendors must propose a region strategy and data residency approach, but no specific approved/prohibited regions or sovereignty rules are stated in the main document. See [PAGE 6].
All Kohler RFP information is confidential and must be used solely for responding to the RFP. See [PAGE 11].
Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share Kohler information with third parties without prior written consent. See [PAGE 11].
Selected OT-adjacent workloads are in scope only for read-only or controlled integration scenarios, creating operational and security boundary risk. See [PAGE 6].
Detailed legal terms, compliance requirements, and SLAs are deferred to subsequent phases after vendor selection. See [PAGE 11].

similar_deals.title: Kohler Co. — Project Polaris / Hybrid Hosting, DCaaS, Cloud Evaluation
similar_deals.industry: Manufacturing, enterprise IT, hospitality, digital innovation, infrastructure hosting
similar_deals.deal_size_usd: Inferred downstream opportunity approximately $15M-$50M over 3-5 years; Phase 0 only not a committed contract
similar_deals.why_relevant: This is the exact RFP profile: Kohler, Wisconsin/global manufacturing estate, Wisconsin Data Center, SAP/non-SAP hosting, VMware, cloud/DCaaS/hybrid evaluation, data center exit considerations, resilience, DR, managed services and Phase 0 decision package.
similar_deals.key_lesson: The strongest proposal pattern is a vendor-neutral Hosting Option Control Tower / 12-week Hosting Decision Lab with open-book USD TCO, workload-by-workload placement, landing-zone blueprints, compliance evidence, migration waves, rollback gates, vendor coexistence RACI and FinOps guardrails. However, the reusable content must explicitly add Prod/Test/Dev segregation and attach certification evidence.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation — UK
similar_deals.why_relevant: Closest cautionary comparable on industry, size, duration and enterprise IT transformation scope. It directly informs Polaris compliance and disqualification risk.
similar_deals.key_lesson: A strong technical and certification posture can still lose if a local entity or compliance appendix requirement is missed. For Polaris, every RFP requirement, appendix, certification request and template instruction must be mapped explicitly.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation — Germany
similar_deals.why_relevant: Strong manufacturing analogue in the target size range, with lessons applicable to enterprise hosting/transformation and operational mobilization.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Manufacturing-sector credentials and fastest mobilization were decisive. Polaris should include named manufacturing references, sector-specific workload understanding and team readiness within two weeks.

similar_deals.title: SAP S/4HANA Global Rollout — Germany/USA/Mexico
similar_deals.why_relevant: Larger than the inferred Polaris range, but highly relevant to global manufacturing, U.S. footprint, SAP workload resilience and migration credibility.
similar_deals.key_lesson: SAP/global manufacturing credibility, zero-downtime methodology, follow-the-sun operations and named case studies can overcome major competitors. Polaris should emphasize SAP/non-SAP hosting resilience, DR and migration continuity.

similar_deals.title: Retail IT Transformation — France
similar_deals.why_relevant: Different industry and slightly below target size, but relevant to mid-sized multi-year IT transformation pricing and commercial positioning.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Best price-to-quality ratio with a balanced shore model was decisive. Polaris pricing should be transparent and modular across Phase 0, landing zone, migration, hosting, DR, support and managed services.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 44%. Evidence supports a credible but not dominant pursuit: the internal proposal corpus contains an exact Kohler Polaris WIP match with strong reusable positioning, pricing anchors ($279,533.42-$595,000 for Phase 0 and $30,667,392 for a 36-month downstream modernization), and solution patterns closely aligned to the RFP. The deal corpus includes relevant manufacturing wins, especially DEAL-089 and DEAL-003, showing that manufacturing credentials, SAP credibility, rapid mobilization and follow-the-sun delivery can win similar transformation work. However, the closest high-fit comparable, DEAL-056, was lost on a compliance appendix miss, which is directly relevant because this RFP has multiple disqualifier-style requirements: mandatory response template, defined submission channel/timeline, no simple Yes/No answers, certification evidence, DCaaS attestations and USD pricing. Public procurement data is not available, so there is no ground-truth evidence on competitor award patterns or price-to-win; therefore competitor assumptions cannot be validated and probability should not be raised. The current proposal evidence also has fixable but material gaps around explicit Prod/Test/Dev segregation, DCaaS-specific attestations and inventory-based sizing confirmation. Confidence level: medium-low, based on two useful intelligence layers plus no public procurement validation.
capability_gaps: Current reusable Kohler proposal content does not explicitly address Prod/Test/Dev/UAT/DR/sandbox environment segregation, while the RFP requires segregation for Prod/Test/Dev. This must be added with subscription/account/project structure, network segmentation, IAM separation, data masking, change controls, logging, backup and DR separation.

Certification and compliance evidence is a gating risk: the RFP requires evidence of certifications and DCaaS compliance attestations. The available proposal intelligence references a compliance evidence matrix, but does not confirm that actual ISO/SOC/regional/industry certification artifacts and DCaaS partner attestations are attached.

DCaaS-specific response depth is not yet proven in the provided proposal content. The strongest reusable content is cloud/hybrid/Azure-first and vendor-neutral, but the RFP requires vendors to respond across Cloud, DCaaS and Hybrid where applicable and to state assumptions clearly.

Inventory-based sizing is mandatory, but the provided intelligence does not include confirmation that the attached Kohler inventory dataset has been fully normalized, dependency-mapped and used as the pricing baseline. Any generic sizing would be non-compliant.
Public procurement validation is unavailable, so pricing and competitor assumptions cannot be validated against actual government award data. This weakens confidence in price-to-win assumptions.
recommended_win_themes: Vendor-neutral Hosting Option Control Tower: position Polaris as a defensible 12-week Hosting Decision Lab that compares public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration workload by workload, rather than forcing a cloud-first or DCaaS-first answer.

Manufacturing-safe hybrid modernization: use manufacturing-specific credibility from comparable wins to emphasize SAP/non-SAP resilience, plant and OT-adjacent workload safety, low-disruption migration, rollback gates, hypercare and protection of production operations.

Evidence-led architecture and compliance: make the response auditable with explicit diagrams, landing-zone patterns, IAM integration, environment segregation, connectivity, DR tiers, data residency, compliance evidence and a requirement-by-requirement compliance matrix.

Fast mobilization with execution continuity: mirror lessons from DEAL-089 and DEAL-069 by committing a named team ready within two weeks, a 12-week Phase 0 plan, and a clear path into a 36-month co-managed hybrid modernization program if Kohler proceeds.

Open-book USD TCO and FinOps transparency: separate Phase 0, setup, migration, hosting, support and managed services pricing; show SAP versus non-SAP economics; include unit pricing, assumptions, chargeback/showback, optimization levers and fee-at-risk governance tied to availability and performance.
risk_factors: No public procurement data is available, so there is no ground-truth validation of competitor win rates or awarded pricing for this exact type of work. Confidence is therefore medium-low despite strong internal intelligence.
The opportunity is currently a Phase 0 evaluation, not a committed downstream transformation contract. The inferred $15M-$50M value may not materialize unless Kohler proceeds after the evaluation.

The closest high-fit historical comparable, DEAL-056, was a $31M/5-year manufacturing IT transformation loss caused by a missed compliance appendix requirement. This is highly relevant because the Kohler RFP has multiple disqualifier-style administrative and evidence requirements.
Mandatory response-template, submission-channel, USD pricing and certification-evidence requirements create avoidable disqualification risk.
The past proposal intelligence is an exact Kohler Polaris WIP match, but it is not evidence of a prior win. It should guide solution structure, not be treated as proof of incumbent advantage.
The proposal content must be strengthened for DCaaS-specific compliance attestations and explicit Prod/Test/Dev segregation; these are not optional details in the RFP.
Competitor position is unknown. In the absence of public award data or incumbent intelligence, win probability should not be inflated.
must_fix_before_bidding: Build a strict compliance matrix covering every RFP requirement, appendix, template field, certification request, pricing-currency instruction, submission channel and due-date requirement. The closest comparable loss, DEAL-056, was lost because a compliance appendix requirement was missed.
Use the required Vendor Response template only, avoid simple Yes/No answers, and include section-level evidence references for each mandatory requirement.

Add a dedicated target-state architecture pack with diagrams for Cloud, DCaaS and Hybrid scenarios, including landing zone deployment model, application deployment model, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, IAM, hybrid connectivity, region/data residency, HA/DR, backup, monitoring, logging and security tooling.

Attach actual certification evidence and DCaaS compliance attestations, including applicable ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II or equivalent evidence, plus DCaaS facility/provider attestations if DCaaS is proposed.

Base all indicative pricing on Kohler’s provided inventory dataset and explicitly list data gaps, sizing assumptions, workload placement assumptions, migration-wave assumptions and USD-only pricing across Phase 0, setup, migration, hosting, support and managed services.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: medium confidence: HCLTech has strong global similar-scope signals, including FY26 $9.3B new-deal TCV, a $100M+ AI Factory / next-generation AI data center program, hybrid cloud and Digital Foundation services, and a $473M five-year AI-led technology-partner engagement. However, likelihood is not marked high because no Wisconsin-specific job cluster, Wisconsin hosting win, or public HCLTech Wisconsin infrastructure-hosting contract was found. Sources: HCLTech Apr. 21 2026 FY26 release; HCLTech U.S. careers/presence pages; no public procurement data available.
competitors.predicted_positioning: AI-led hybrid cloud and digital foundation partner: HCLTech will likely position around enterprise-scale managed infrastructure, hybrid/multicloud operations, AI-enabled operations, cyber resilience, Google Cloud/Red Hat/Intel ecosystem credibility, and manufacturing enterprise IT. Source evidence: HCLTech Digital Foundation services; Apr. 21 2026 FY26 release citing $620M annualized Advanced AI revenue and $100M+ AI Factory / next-generation AI data center program; May-Jun. 2026 Red Hat, Google Cloud, and Intel AI infrastructure announcements.
competitors.predicted_price_range_usd: Estimated $18M-$36M over 3-5 years, medium-low confidence because no public contract price anchor exists. Margin/revenue grounding: FY26 revenue/employee = $14.7B / 227,000+ employees = about $64.8k per employee/year. FY26 EBIT margin = 17.2%, so implied cost per revenue employee = $64.8k * (1 - 0.172) = about $53.6k, and pricing floor formula is cost / (1 - 0.172). A $18M-$36M bid implies delivery cost of about $14.9M-$29.8M at HCLTech's reported margin. Source: HCLTech Apr. 21 2026 FY26 release. No public procurement data available; no recent contract wins in Wisconsin found.
their_strengths: Large global IT services scale: $14.7B FY26 revenue and 227,000+ employees across 60 countries. Source: HCLTech Apr. 21 2026 FY26 release.
Healthy operating margin for price flexibility: FY26 EBIT margin 17.2%, 17.9% excluding restructuring costs, with FY27 EBIT margin guidance of 17.5%-18.5%. Source: HCLTech Apr. 21 2026 FY26 release.

Relevant infrastructure/AI proof: a global technology major selected HCLTech for another $100M+ AI Factory program including next-generation AI data centers. Source: HCLTech Apr. 21 2026 FY26 release.

Hybrid cloud and managed infrastructure portfolio through Digital Foundation services covering hybrid cloud, networks, workplace, service management, cyber resilience, monitoring, and continuous operations. Source: HCLTech Digital Foundation pages.
Current AI infrastructure partnerships with Red Hat, Google Cloud, and Intel. Sources: HCLTech May 14, Jun. 8, and Jun. 18 2026 announcements.
their_weaknesses: No recent contract wins in Wisconsin found; no public HCLTech Wisconsin infrastructure-hosting contract found. Source: provided web intelligence; no public procurement data available.
No verified active Wisconsin-specific HCLTech infrastructure-hosting hiring found; U.S. careers evidence exists but not Wisconsin-specific. Source: HCLTech U.S. careers/presence pages.
Hospitality-specific current evidence is weak: no 2026 HCLTech hospitality-specific deal was found in the provided research. Source: HCLTech web intelligence summary.

May over-index on AI messaging versus Kohler's practical Phase 0 requirements for architecture, DCaaS compliance attestations, USD pricing, and detailed template compliance. Source: HCLTech 2026 AI announcements and RFP disqualifier summary.
how_to_beat_them: Exploit the Wisconsin gap: require named Wisconsin/Midwest onsite architecture, migration, network, security, and incident-response leads in the Vendor Response template; provided evidence shows HCLTech has U.S. hiring and presence across 19 states but no verified Wisconsin-specific hosting hiring or Wisconsin hosting contract. Source: HCLTech U.S. careers/presence pages; no public procurement data available.

Do not let them win on AI branding alone: force the evaluation back to Kohler's concrete Phase 0 requirements: landing zone model, application deployment model, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity integration, hybrid connectivity, region/data residency, DR/HA/scalability, and certification evidence. HCLTech's latest public push is AI Factory, Red Hat AI infrastructure, Google Cloud/Intel AI Innovation Zones. Source: HCLTech Apr. 21, May 14, Jun. 8, and Jun. 18 2026 announcements.

Counter with a more transparent USD commercial model: open-book setup, migration, run, storage, backup, DR, network, security, and exit pricing. No public procurement prices were available for HCLTech in this pursuit, so pricing confidence is not contract-anchored. Source: provided public contract section states no public procurement data available.

Demand DCaaS compliance attestations early; make HCLTech provide facility-level, control-level evidence rather than general global certifications. The RFP makes certification evidence and DCaaS attestations disqualification risks. Source: RFP pages 7-9 as summarized in prompt.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: medium confidence: DXC has strong offering relevance through Cloud & Infrastructure Services, 1,300+ cloud/infrastructure customers, DXC OASIS, and Private Cloud+; however, no official Wisconsin-specific infrastructure job posting and no Wisconsin hosting contract were found. Sources: DXC Cloud & Infrastructure pages; DXC Apr. 28 and Jul. 1 2026 announcements; DXC careers results; no public procurement data available.
competitors.predicted_positioning: AI-era managed infrastructure and hybrid private cloud modernization: expect DXC to lead with DXC Private Cloud+, Dell-backed private cloud, DXC OASIS intelligent orchestration, ServiceNow partnership, OpenAI alliance, manufacturing IT/OT experience, and enterprise managed infrastructure. Sources: DXC Apr. 7, Apr. 28, Jun. 11, and Jul. 1 2026 announcements; DXC manufacturing and cloud/infrastructure pages.

solution_options.name: Premium Hybrid Resilience and Transformation Program
solution_options.description: Premium lowest-risk full enterprise hybrid transformation and managed-hosting option. Includes a Kohler-owned Polaris Architecture and Commercial Evidence Pack, deep dependency mapping across SAP and non-SAP workloads, stronger dual-region DR/HA patterns, larger dedicated 24/7 operations coverage, broader migration support, DCaaS compliance evidence, and more extensive automation and observability.
key_components: 12-week Polaris Hosting Decision Lab included in the full program with outputs reusable by Kohler
Full workload placement assessment across Cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration patterns
Enterprise landing-zone architecture covering account/subscription structure, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, IAM, network segmentation, logging, backup and DR separation
Premium HA/DR architecture with more aggressive resilience testing and dual-region design patterns
Kohler-owned Terraform/IaC repository and vendor-neutral architecture pack
Dedicated 24/7 managed services model with expanded SRE, security, DBA, network, platform and service-delivery coverage
AI-enabled observability, FinOps guardrails, ITSM integration and compliance evidence matrix
More comprehensive migration factory for SAP-adjacent, database, middleware, file storage and COTS/custom application waves
solution_options.rationale: Best for lowest execution risk and highest resilience. Pricing math: People $16.368M, based on approximately 10 Phase 0 FTE for 3 months, 32 transition/migration FTE for 12 months, and 24 steady-state managed-services FTE for 45 months using blended global rates; Infrastructure $9.800M, using live AWS/Azure unit prices scaled to approximately 430 enterprise VM/database/security/monitoring equivalents plus storage, logs, data transfer, Defender, Azure high-compute, backup, DR, licenses, hybrid connectivity and DCaaS/private-hosting uplift; Migration/Transformation $4.500M; Governance/Compliance/Tooling $2.000M. Subtotal $32.668M + 15% contingency $4.900M + 22% margin on cost $8.265M = $45.833M total over 60 months. This is within the inferred $15M-$50M opportunity but above the competitor price-to-win band, so it should be positioned only if Kohler prioritizes risk reduction and resilience over price.

solution_options.name: Balanced Polaris Hybrid Hosting and Managed Services Program
solution_options.description: Balanced recommended full-program option for a right-fit hybrid target state. It prices the complete scope: Phase 0 evaluation, inventory normalization, target-state architecture, landing-zone design/deployment, workload placement, migration waves, hybrid/DCaaS integration, security, observability, backup/DR and 5-year managed services. This option is designed to win within the $19M-$30M competitor price-to-win range while preserving realistic delivery margin.
key_components: Fixed-fee Polaris Hosting Decision Lab included and creditable into implementation
Three executable target-state options: Cloud, DCaaS and Hybrid, built from Kohler's inventory baseline and stated assumptions
Multi-account/multi-subscription landing-zone model with Prod/Test/Dev segregation, IAM integration, network segmentation, logging and DR separation
Hybrid connectivity, region/data-residency, HA/DR, backup and observability design
Workload placement scorecards for SAP/non-SAP, COTS, internally developed, database, middleware, file and OT-adjacent workloads
Migration-wave factory with dependency mapping, testing, cutover, rollback and parallel-run planning
Managed services with global blended delivery across service management, SRE/cloud ops, DBA, network, security, backup/DR and FinOps
Open-book USD unit price book for setup, run, migration, DR, security, compliance, change and exit services
solution_options.rationale: Recommended because it aligns to the inferred opportunity size, HCLTech's prior Kohler downstream pricing anchors, and the competitor price-to-win range. Pricing math: People $10.488M, based on approximately 8 Phase 0 FTE for 3 months at advisory blended rates, 24 transition/migration FTE for 12 months, and 18 steady-state managed-services FTE for 45 months using a global blended delivery model; Infrastructure $6.200M, built from live AWS/Azure rates scaled to enterprise size, including approximately 100 AWS m5.xlarge, 50 m5.2xlarge, 20 m5.4xlarge, 20 r5.xlarge, 30 c5.xlarge, 60 t3.medium, 26 RDS instances, 250TB S3, 45TB RDS storage, 25TB/month data transfer, 20TB/month CloudWatch logs, 300 Defender nodes and 2 Azure EC48 equivalents, plus hybrid connectivity, backup, licensing, DR and DCaaS/private-hosting uplift; Migration/Transformation $2.800M; Governance/Tooling/Compliance $1.200M. Subtotal $20.688M + 12% contingency $2.483M + 20% margin on cost $4.634M = $27.805M total over 60 months.

solution_options.name: Lean Hybrid Foundation and Managed Services Program
solution_options.description: Lean lowest-cost option that covers the full program but narrows the implementation path, uses a more shared delivery model, limits modernization, assumes higher incumbent/client participation, and focuses on critical workloads first. It remains above the minimum enterprise-deal threshold and inside the low end of the price-to-win range, but carries higher delivery, resilience and scope-management risk.
key_components: Phase 0 Hosting Decision Lab with tighter workshop schedule and fewer architecture iterations
Prioritized hybrid target state for critical workloads with limited alternatives modeling
Landing-zone minimum viable design covering Prod/Test/Dev segregation, IAM, connectivity, monitoring, backup and DR controls
Selective migration factory focused on highest-value and highest-risk workload waves first
Greater reliance on incumbent/client teams for inventory validation, dependency confirmation and application testing
Shared 24/7 managed-services pool rather than larger dedicated operations team
Standard observability and FinOps dashboards with fewer custom automations
Open-book price book with stricter assumptions and change-control triggers for inventory growth, migration complexity and DR scope
solution_options.rationale: Lowest-cost compliant full-program posture, suitable only if Kohler accepts tighter assumptions, more retained responsibility and reduced transformation depth. Pricing math: People $7.800M, based on approximately 6 Phase 0 FTE for 3 months, 18 transition/migration FTE for 9 months, and 14 steady-state managed-services FTE for 48 months using a shared/offshore-heavy delivery model; Infrastructure $4.800M, using live AWS/Azure rates scaled to a smaller cloud migration footprint of approximately 185 VM equivalents plus RDS, storage, logs, data transfer, Defender and limited Azure high-compute capacity, with added backup, licensing, hybrid connectivity and DCaaS/private-hosting uplift; Migration/Transformation $2.000M; Governance/Tooling/Compliance $0.700M. Subtotal $15.300M + 10% contingency $1.530M + 18% margin on cost $3.029M = $19.859M total over 60 months. This is commercially aggressive and within the low end of the $19M-$30M price-to-win band, but risk is higher because scope, migration complexity and inventory growth could quickly consume contingency.

pricing.pricing_structure: Full-program commercial model over 60 months: fixed-fee Phase 0 Polaris Hosting Decision Lab included and creditable into execution; milestone-based migration and landing-zone implementation; recurring monthly managed hosting, DCaaS/hybrid operations, security, observability, backup/DR and governance; open-book USD unit-rate book for compute, storage, database, monitoring, migration waves, DR, security tooling, exit and change volumes.

cost_drivers.category: People / managed services: Phase 0, transition, SRE/cloud ops, security, DBA, network, service delivery, FinOps
Infrastructure: live AWS/Azure cloud cost scaled to enterprise size plus hybrid connectivity, backup, licensing, DR and DCaaS/private-hosting uplift
Migration / transformation: inventory normalization, dependency mapping, landing zones, migration waves, testing, cutover and rollback
Governance / tooling / compliance: ITSM integration, reporting, compliance evidence, architecture governance, FinOps and security controls
Contingency at 12% of pre-margin delivery cost
Margin at 20% on total cost after contingency

pricing.competitive_rationale: Recommended Option B is anchored to HCLTech's prior Kohler Polaris pricing intelligence: Phase 0 advisory benchmarks of $279,533-$595,000 and downstream transformation/managed-services scenarios of $30.667M and $35.704M, with an inferred full opportunity of $15M-$50M over 3-5 years. No public contract award data was available, so market validation is limited. The recommended $27.805M full-program price is inside the competitor price-to-win band of $19M-$30M, below likely Accenture premium pricing, and avoids a margin-risk low bid. Live cloud pricing was used for the cloud infrastructure slice and scaled to an enterprise estate rather than single-VM pricing: Option B assumes approximately 280 VM equivalents plus RDS, storage, logging, security, data transfer, and Azure high-compute capacity, then adds hybrid connectivity, backup, licensing, and DCaaS/private-hosting uplift. Math for recommended Option B: People $10.488M + Infrastructure $6.200M + Migration/Transformation $2.800M + Governance/Tooling $1.200M = $20.688M subtotal; contingency at 12% = $2.483M; margin at 20% on cost = $4.634M; total = $27.805M.

pursuit_plan.strategy: Treat Project Polaris as a strategic Phase 0 shaping opportunity, not just a hosting quote. Win by giving Kohler a credible, evidence-backed decision framework comparing Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options while demonstrating that the bidder can safely execute the eventual data center exit with manufacturing-grade resilience, compliance, and cost control. The response should be highly structured, template-compliant, assumption-transparent, and tailored to Kohler’s global manufacturing, hospitality, and enterprise IT environment.
pursuit_plan.win_strategy: Provide the most credible and transparent decision framework for Kohler’s data center exit: a manufacturing-aware Cloud/DCaaS/Hybrid comparison that ties architecture, compliance evidence, migration risk reduction, resilience, operating model, and USD cost transparency directly to Kohler’s inventory and business continuity needs.
pursuit_plan.pricing_approach: Use a hybrid competitive/value-based pricing approach with rigorous cost transparency. Because no evaluation weights are provided and cost transparency is explicitly evaluated, price should be structured as comparable scenarios rather than a single opaque total: Cloud-first, DCaaS-first, and Hybrid target-state options with one-time migration/setup, recurring run costs, managed services, connectivity, security, backup, DR, tooling, and optional services separated. Lead with a competitive baseline for core hosting and managed services to stay credible against Kyndryl/DXC/TCS/HCLTech, then use value-based differentiation for resilience, governance automation, manufacturing-aware migration controls, FinOps, and reduced transition risk. Include assumptions, unit rates, sensitivity ranges, reserved/committed-use options, and clear USD pricing aligned to the Vendor Response template.
key_risks: Phase 0 ambiguity: the RFP requests evaluation of hosting options but does not commit to a downstream implementation contract, so the pursuit must avoid over-investing without shaping the next phase and securing a clear path to execution.

Mandatory template and submission compliance risk: failure to use the Vendor Response template, provide detailed narrative answers, submit via the correct channel by 26 June 2026, or quote all pricing in USD could materially damage or disqualify the bid.

Certification and attestation risk: the RFP requires evidence of applicable hosting certifications and explicit DCaaS compliance attestations, but the standards are not named; the team must proactively map and evidence likely applicable standards.

Inventory dependency risk: sizing, architecture, and pricing must be based on the provided application/infrastructure inventory, but gaps are expected; unclear dependencies, performance baselines, storage growth, licensing, and RTO/RPO targets could undermine credibility.

Hybrid connectivity and manufacturing uptime risk: Kohler has enterprise and manufacturing/OT-adjacent environments where latency, segmentation, read-only/controlled integration, and plant availability constraints may make generic cloud migration approaches risky.

Cost credibility risk: evaluation includes cost and commercial transparency, so opaque bundled pricing, unstated assumptions, underestimated egress/connectivity/DR costs, or weak cloud cost governance could lose the deal.

Competitor positioning risk: large incumbents such as Kyndryl, DXC, Accenture, HCLTech, and TCS may have stronger legacy hosting credentials, existing relationships, or aggressive managed services pricing.

Data residency and global operations risk: Kohler’s global footprint requires a region strategy that satisfies residency, resilience, and operational support needs across jurisdictions, but detailed requirements are not supplied in the main RFP.

Future contracting uncertainty: legal terms, SLAs, service delivery frameworks, compliance obligations, and contract duration are deferred to later phases, creating risk in commercial assumptions and solution commitments.
SAP/non-SAP integration risk: even though detailed SAP scope is not fully specified, SAP-adjacent dependencies can drive hosting placement, latency, DR, licensing, and migration sequencing decisions.

gaps: Competitor intelligence is materially incomplete in the supplied output: only HCLTech is visible, while the most important likely competitor/incumbent, Cognizant, is not analyzed despite client intel showing a multi-year Cognizant extension covering AI, cloud, infrastructure and cyber operations.

Competitor pricing for HCLTech is weakly grounded. The revenue-per-employee/EBIT-margin formula is not a reliable deal-price anchor for a Kohler hosting/DCaaS/cloud evaluation, and no comparable contract, managed hosting benchmark, or TCV-per-scope evidence is cited.

Competitor weaknesses appear partly generic or low-impact: 'no Wisconsin contract found' is not a strong vulnerability for a global enterprise IT outsourcing bid unless local hosting or Wisconsin presence is an RFP scoring factor.

Win intelligence has useful internal comparables, but the strongest comparable is the current WIP RFP itself, which should not materially calibrate win probability. The provided excerpt also does not show a clear win-probability assessment or disqualifier-adjusted probability.

The outputs appear truncated, especially WIN_INTEL, CLIENT_INTEL and COMPETITOR. If the source objects are actually complete, this may be a display issue; if not, the proposal team lacks full reasoning and recommendations.

Client intelligence is directionally strong and specific, but several web signals are summarized without precise citations/URLs in the provided output. This is acceptable for speed but should be tightened if used directly in an executive win theme.
strengths: Client intelligence contains real Kohler-specific signals, including Cognizant partnership, Kohler Energy carve-out complexity, SAP ECC, Salesforce/CRM/analytics integration, Azure/data engineering signals, connected-product security and observability needs.

Unstated needs are mostly insightful and actionable: workload placement, dependency mapping, hybrid decision framework, AI-enabled observability, cyber-by-design controls and incumbent integration are all directly relevant to Project Polaris.

Win-intel internal comparables include relevant manufacturing transformation lessons around compliance appendices, mobilization speed, SAP/global manufacturing credibility and zero-downtime methodology.
RFP disqualifiers are clearly identified and should help shape the compliance matrix, response template discipline, certification evidence appendix and USD pricing guardrails.

agentic_decisions.reasoning: Treat Project Polaris as a strategic Phase 0 shaping opportunity, not just a hosting quote. Win by giving Kohler a credible, evidence-backed decision framework comparing Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options while demonstrating that the bidder can safely execute the eventual data center exit with manufacturing-grade resilience, compliance, and cost control. The response should be highly structured, template-compliant, assumption-transparent, and tailored to Kohler’s global manufacturing, hospitality, and enterprise IT environment.

The client intelligence is good enough to support a tailored narrative: it identifies specific Kohler signals around Cognizant, cloud/infrastructure/cyber, application carve-out complexity, SAP ECC, observability, Azure/data engineering and connected-product security. The win-intel comparable set is also directionally useful. However, the competitor work has a material blind spot: Cognizant is likely the central competitive threat because it appears to be the incumbent/strategic partner for exactly the relevant domains. Without assessing Cognizant's likely positioning, lock-in advantages, transition friction, pricing posture and vulnerabilities, the proposal may choose the wrong win strategy. HCLTech analysis alone is insufficient for a $15M-$50M enterprise hosting/DCaaS/cloud decision. Pricing evidence is also not strong enough yet. This warrants a focused re-run/deepening of the competitor agent rather than a full restart of all agents.
